caveats: a: No AI task-exposure study specific to NAICS 333924 was found.
a: Manufacturers with software-led fleet services may have more exposed office work than fabrication-focused niche producers.
rho: The adoption evidence is not denominator-matched to lower-middle-market industrial-truck manufacturers.
rho: Product telemetry does not automatically imply that internal workflows are ready for AI.
e: Large-OEM service mix may overstate recurring-service penetration among small manufacturers.
e: Dealer-delivered service may not belong economically to the manufacturer and must be tested firm by firm.
s5: The observed acquisitions were outside the US and include adjacent manufacturing capabilities.
s5: No direct control-transfer rate for eligible firms was available.
q: Public disclosures describe a global OEM rather than eligible US firms.
q: Retention likely differs sharply between custom lifecycle contracts and standardized equipment or parts sales.
d5: Company bookings and backlog mix price, share, geography, and product mix with physical demand.
d5: The eligible recurring-service basket may be more stable than new-equipment shipments.
o: The accountable-operator share is not directly observed.
o: Dealer networks can retain the service relationship even when the manufacturer remains necessary for equipment supply.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Manufacturers sell equipment and parts directly or through dealers to warehouses, logistics operators, retailers, food and beverage companies, manufacturers, and other industrial users. Eligible firms add recurring revenue through full-maintenance leases, extended warranties, fleet management, subscriptions, licensing, remote services, rentals, parts, and application support tied to an installed equipment base.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure dealers and rental companies without in-scope manufacturing, motor-vehicle trailer manufacturers, farm and construction tractor manufacturers, captive internal units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, firms outside the lower-middle-market band, and manufacturers limited to one-time equipment sales without recurring external service relationships.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of forklifts, pallet trucks, stackers, tow tractors, truck-type industrial trailers, portable loading docks, attachments, and related industrial vehicles that provide recurring or repeat outsourced service to external customers through maintenance contracts, fleet management, equipment rental support, parts programs, application engineering, remote services, or lifecycle support.

a.bridge: NIST describes AI use cases across US manufacturing rather than measuring the wage-weighted task mix of industrial-truck manufacturers. I map predictive maintenance, vision inspection, demand forecasting, production scheduling, document processing, and design assistance to fabrication, assembly, engineering, quality, quoting, parts, fleet analytics, and customer-support work. Because welding, machining, assembly, testing, field repair, and material handling remain physical, the broad manufacturing evidence may overstate substitutable wage share.
caveats: No AI task-exposure study specific to NAICS 333924 was found.
Manufacturers with software-led fleet services may have more exposed office work than fabrication-focused niche producers.
a.change_evidence: Occupation-level payroll and task-time studies from representative equipment manufacturers, separated between production, engineering, administration, parts, and field service, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: AI can reduce labor in engineering support, quoting, scheduling, inspection, maintenance analysis, inventory, technical documentation, fleet analytics, and customer service. The equipment itself and its lifecycle service still require substantial physical fabrication, assembly, testing, installation, and repair. [S2] [S3] [S4]

d5.bridge: Hyster-Yale reports a recent global lift-truck downturn, lower backlog, improving bookings, deferred customer purchases, and replacement demand from aging equipment. These company indicators are nominal and global rather than constant-price US quantity for the eligible service basket. I use current weakness in the low case and replacement, warehouse automation, and installed-base service in the base and high cases. A single OEM may understate demand in segments where it has weaker product coverage and overstate rebound if its bookings reflect share recovery.
caveats: Company bookings and backlog mix price, share, geography, and product mix with physical demand.
The eligible recurring-service basket may be more stable than new-equipment shipments.
d5.change_evidence: Industrial Truck Association US unit shipments and orders, segmented by truck class and paired with installed-base age and service revenue, would materially change this ratio.
d5.rationale: Industrial trucks are essential to warehousing, logistics, retail, food, manufacturing, and durable-goods operations. Capital purchases are cyclical and recently weakened, but an aging fleet, delayed replacement, automation, connected services, and recurring parts and maintenance support a moderate five-year recovery. [S2] [S5]

caveats: Large-OEM service mix may overstate recurring-service penetration among small manufacturers.
Dealer-delivered service may not belong economically to the manufacturer and must be tested firm by firm.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of lower-middle-market manufacturers, with revenue and contracts coded for maintenance, fleet management, subscriptions, parts programs, warranties, rentals, and recurring application support, would directly determine eligibility.
e.rationale: The industry is primarily equipment manufacturing, but an installed fleet naturally creates recurring parts, maintenance, warranty, rental, fleet-management, and technical-support relationships. A major OEM reports substantial service and parts revenue and markets maintenance leases and fleet services, supporting a meaningful eligible subset; pure build-to-ship manufacturers remain outside the lens. [S1] [S2] [S5]

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 333924 ($2,527,016,312) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 333924 ($14,026,389,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $459,701 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 333924); class <5: 76 firms, avg revenue/firm $697,750 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $136,899 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,091,683 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $410,388 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,276,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $642,849 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 69 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,684,174 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,488,635 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 38 firms, avg revenue/firm $64,997,053 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,752,422 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 31 firms, avg revenue/firm $335,007,516 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $65,728,475 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 69

caveats: The accountable-operator share is not directly observed.
Dealer networks can retain the service relationship even when the manufacturer remains necessary for equipment supply.
o.change_evidence: Customer and dealer interviews quantifying self-maintenance, direct imports, third-party fleet software, autonomous-equipment sourcing, and manufacturer service attachment would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Year-five demand still requires an accountable manufacturer and service organization to engineer, fabricate, assemble, certify, maintain, and support physical equipment. Automation changes the product and enables remote service rather than eliminating the operator; modest leakage can occur through imports, dealer self-performance, customer maintenance, standardized platforms, or software vendors capturing fleet-management work. [S1] [S2] [S5]

caveats: Public disclosures describe a global OEM rather than eligible US firms.
Retention likely differs sharply between custom lifecycle contracts and standardized equipment or parts sales.
q.change_evidence: Maintenance agreements, renewal histories, service gross-margin bridges, dealer economics, bid win-loss data, and customer interviews on total-cost-of-ownership procurement would materially narrow the range.
q.rationale: Maintenance contracts, fleet-management services, subscriptions, and application-specific equipment can preserve savings through switching costs and installed-base knowledge. Retention is reduced by intense OEM and dealer competition, transparent equipment and parts pricing, customer procurement, cost-plus engineering work, contract renewal, and pressure to offset steel, component, and tariff costs. [S2] [S5]

rho.bridge: NIST manufacturing use cases and barriers, Census business adoption evidence, and Hyster-Yale's current software, telemetry, and remote-service direction concern broader or larger populations than eligible US firms. I translate them into five-year implementation, discounting for legacy factory systems, product safety, field-service integration, cybersecurity, dealer workflows, capital limits, and skills. Large OEM progress may overstate implementation speed in lower-middle-market firms.
caveats: The adoption evidence is not denominator-matched to lower-middle-market industrial-truck manufacturers.
Product telemetry does not automatically imply that internal workflows are ready for AI.
rho.change_evidence: A survey of eligible manufacturers covering machine connectivity, ERP and MES use, connected installed base, AI pilots, dealer data access, implementation budgets, and production deployment would materially refine the estimate.
rho.rationale: Vision inspection, predictive maintenance, planning, quoting, knowledge retrieval, parts forecasting, and remote support can be deployed incrementally, while connected equipment creates useful data. Legacy machinery, fragmented dealer data, safety-critical products, cyber risk, workforce readiness, and integration cost constrain implementation. [S2] [S3] [S4] [S5]

s5.bridge: Hyster-Yale disclosed acquisitions of small manufacturing operations in adjacent lift-truck and machining activities, including a current-year transaction, but this is not a five-year transfer rate for eligible US lower-middle-market firms. I use the transactions as evidence that strategic control transfers occur and that small manufacturing assets can be integrated, then discount because the targets were outside the US and the eligible-firm denominator and owner-age distribution are unknown. Strategic activity by a global OEM may overstate buyer access for isolated niche firms.
caveats: The observed acquisitions were outside the US and include adjacent manufacturing capabilities.
No direct control-transfer rate for eligible firms was available.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, or BizBuySell transactions mapped to NAICS 333924, combined with owner-age and succession interviews for eligible US manufacturers, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Recent small manufacturing acquisitions demonstrate executable strategic demand around lift-truck attachments and production capabilities. No direct US succession cohort or denominator-matched deal series was found, so the probability remains a bounded proxy. [S2]

principal_driver: A durable installed equipment base creates recurring parts, maintenance, fleet-data, and remote-service workflows where AI can raise productivity.
principal_weakness: Independent dealers often control lifecycle service, leaving many manufacturers outside the recurring outsourced-service lens.
